1._: Vocabulary used by this convention. Each position defines one term that recurs in the procedural branches and the data-model shapes. Look up a term by walking its position; read the whole branch for full context. The vocabulary is intentionally small — the convention is one paragraph of new ideas (paywall as gate on sed: registration, ticket as grain envelope, verifier as audit-writer) attached to substrate primitives that already exist.
1.1: paywall config — a sub-block at position 9 of a `sed:` collective (sed::9) declaring that the collective is paywalled. The sub-block carries five canonical fields at digit positions: issuer (9.1), purchase_url (9.2), face (9.3), scope (9.4), verifier (9.5). A collective without a paywall config sub-block is open and unchanged. The sub-block is metadata — bsp-mcp does not interpret it; it changes neither read nor write semantics on the collective. Position 9 is the canonical metadata slot for sed: collectives per pscale://block-conventions branch 7.3.
1.2: ticket envelope — the string `[ticket face=… scope=… expires=…]` (plus optional fields) written as the issuer-side underscore of a grain. The envelope is what makes an otherwise-ordinary grain a ticket. The grain itself is established via `pscale_grain_reach`; what distinguishes a ticket from any other grain is the envelope text at the issuer's side underscore.
1.3: ticket-grain — an ordinary grain whose issuer-side underscore carries a ticket envelope. The buyer's side carries whatever the buyer's content is, often empty at issuance. Revocations are written as digit children of the issuer's side, not by overwriting the underscore. The substrate cannot tell ticket-grains from any other grain — only the ticket envelope text identifies them.
1.4: registration position — a position in a paywalled `sed:` collective claimed by a buyer. Registrants land at floor-2 digit-path slots (11, 12, ..., 99, 111, ...; sunstone:1.6.4) per the sed: convention; positions 1..9 are structural reservations (paywall config sits at 9). The position's underscore is the buyer's self-description (the face being claimed); the digit-1 child is a string reference of form `grain:<pair_id>:<issuer-side>` pointing at the ticket-grain. The position is write-locked to the buyer's passphrase, which means digit children are also locked — the verifier (a foreign agent) cannot write into the position or any child of it.
1.5: two-write registration ritual — the sequence by which a buyer claims a registration position carrying a grain reference. First call: `pscale_register` with a self-description as `declaration` (server allocates the position, locks it). Second call: `bsp()` write to `<position>.1` placing the grain reference, signed with the buyer's passphrase. The two writes are not atomic; the protocol accepts that a write-1-success / write-2-failure leaves an unbacked registration that the verifier ignores.
1.6: audit log — a `sed:` collective on the verifier's own beach where the verifier writes `[ticket-verified]`, `[ticket-rejected]`, and `[ticket-expired]` envelopes as registrations. One audit collective per calendar month, named `sed:<verifier-bare-id>-audit-<yyyy-mm>`. Decisions live there because the registrant's `sed:` position is locked to the registrant — a foreign verifier cannot write onto it. The audit log is public and walkable; correlation back to a registration is by the `registration=` field in the envelope.
1.7: scope — what a ticket authorises. Three forms: a single frame (`frame:<id>`), a frame pattern (`frame:<prefix>-*`), or a beach (`beach:<host>`). v1 verifiers honour exact-frame and frame-pattern; cross-beach scope is reserved for v2 (rejected with `beach-scope-not-supported-yet`). The collective declares its expected scope at 9.4; the envelope declares the ticket's granted scope; verification checks compatibility.
1.8: face — which CADO face the ticket grants authority for. One of `character`, `author`, `designer`, `observer`. The 9.3 field on the collective is the face that collective represents; the envelope's `face=` field is what the ticket grants. They must match for the ticket to be valid. Observer face requires no membership and is unchanged by this convention — observer reads remain free.
1.9: pscale_attention is negative-floor — when calling `bsp()` against a spindle of length N, `pscale_attention=-N` reads the terminus, `-N+1` reads the disc above (a ring), and so on. The procedural branches in this convention use abstract verbs ("read the underscore", "walk the disc") and concrete `pscale_attention` values are negative; passing positive values to `bsp()` will return `unsupported shape (P_end=-N)` errors. See `pscale://whetstone` branch 2 for the full selection-shape derivation.

2._: Data model — the shapes the convention defines on top of substrate primitives. Five positions: the paywall config sub-block at sed::9, the ticket envelope written at the issuer-side underscore of the grain, the ticket-grain shape with its revocation children, the audit-log envelopes the verifier writes, and the revocation envelope. Each shape's fields are at digit children with the underscore stating what the shape is and where it lives.

1._: Paywall config sub-block — sits at position 9 of a paywalled `sed:` collective. The presence of this sub-block (with its five required fields populated) is what turns an open collective into a paywalled one. The sub-block underscore states the field is a paywall config; digit children carry the field values. All values are strings. The sub-block is metadata; bsp-mcp does not interpret it, and its existence changes neither read nor write semantics on the collective.
1.1: 9.1 issuer — the agent_id of the agent whose grains this collective honours as tickets. Form: `agent:<issuer-id>` or any other agent_id form. A grain established by any other agent is not a valid ticket for this collective even if it carries a well-formed envelope.
1.2: 9.2 purchase_url — the direct URL prospective members visit to obtain a ticket. Required. The client opens this URL when the user clicks the buy affordance; the substrate and client never interpose between buyer and issuer. The URL is the issuer's payment-and-fulfilment surface; the substrate doesn't care what runs there.
1.3: 9.3 face — which CADO face this collective represents. One of `character`, `author`, `designer`. (Observer face requires no membership and isn't paywalled by this convention.) The face declared here must match the `face=` field of any ticket envelope honoured for this collective.
1.4: 9.4 scope — what the ticket must authorise to be valid for this collective. Three forms accepted: exact frame (`frame:<id>`), frame pattern (`frame:<prefix>-*`), or beach (`beach:<host>`; v2-deferred — v1 verifiers reject these). The collective's scope and the envelope's scope are matched per the verification rules.
1.5: 9.5 verifier — the agent_id of the verifier daemon that walks new registrations and writes audit envelopes. Optional; defaults to the value at 9.1 (issuer self-verifies) when omitted. A frame-owner who wants verification independence can run a separate verifier and name it here.

2._: `[ticket …]` envelope — the string written as the issuer-side underscore of a grain. The substrate sees this as ordinary string content on the grain side; the envelope syntax is interpreted by the verifier and the buyer's client. Three required fields and several optional and reserved fields. Field syntax: `key=value` separated by whitespace; values without spaces unquoted, values with spaces quoted with double quotes.
2.1: face — which CADO face this ticket grants. Must match the collective's 9.3 for verification to pass. One of `character`, `author`, `designer`, `observer`.
2.2: scope — where this ticket is valid. Exact frame (`frame:<id>`), frame pattern (`frame:<prefix>-*`), or beach (`beach:<host>`; v2-deferred).
2.3: expires — ISO-8601 timestamp at which the ticket becomes invalid. UTC recommended. Required; v1 verifiers reject envelopes without a parseable expiry.
2.4: tier — optional. Narrows or widens the SMH aperture beyond the face default. One of `soft`, `medium`, `hard`. Honoured by per-face-tier-aware clients; ignored by clients that don't track tier.
2.5: seats — optional. For collectives where one ticket admits multiple agent_ids (rare; useful for guild-style purchases). Integer. v1 verifiers honour or ignore based on collective configuration.
2.6: nonce — optional. The issuer's identifier for deduplication or refund tracking. Opaque to the verifier and the substrate. Often a UUID or short ID.
2.7: credits — RESERVED for future SAND credit integration. v1 verifiers MUST reject any envelope containing a `credits=` field with reason `credits-not-supported`. A grain carrying credits would represent a credit-bearing grant rather than a time-bounded one; reserving the grammar at the protocol layer lets future credit-aware verifiers adopt it without grammar change.

3._: Ticket-grain shape — the grain block as it lives in the substrate when used as a ticket. The issuer-side underscore (a string) carries the ticket envelope. The buyer's side carries whatever content the buyer wrote, often empty. The issuer-side digit children carry revocation envelopes if any were issued. The substrate enforces the per-side write lock as on any grain; the protocol adds no new lock behaviour.
3.1: issuer side — `<issuer-side>` is `1` or `2` depending on lex-ordering of the two agent_ids in the `pair_id` derivation. The issuer learns its side from `grain_address_mine` returned by `pscale_grain_reach`; the issuer communicates this to the buyer in the purchase confirmation so the buyer knows which side to reference.
3.2: issuer-side underscore — carries the ticket envelope as a string. Written once at issuance via `pscale_grain_reach`'s `my_side_content`; never overwritten. If the issuer needs to revoke, the revocation goes as a digit child of the side, not by mutating the underscore.
3.3: issuer-side digit children — carry revocation envelopes. First revocation at `<issuer-side>.1`, subsequent at `.2`, `.3`, etc. Each child is a terminal string of form `[ticket-revoked at=<iso8601> reason=<short>]`. Verifiers walk these children with `pscale_attention=-2` (the side's disc) and treat any `[ticket-revoked]` envelope found as canonical — a grain with a ticket envelope and any later revocation is invalid.
3.4: buyer side — buyer's content; often empty at issuance. The buyer may write here later if the issuer's contract calls for it, but the convention requires nothing of the buyer at this address.

4._: Audit-log envelopes — the strings the verifier writes into its monthly audit collective at `sed:<verifier-bare-id>-audit-<yyyy-mm>`. Each envelope is the `declaration` of a `pscale_register` call by the verifier on its own collective. Three forms — verified, rejected, expired — each carrying correlation fields back to the registration and the grain.
4.1: [ticket-verified by=agent:<verifier-id> at=<iso8601> registration=sed:<collective>:<position> grain=grain:<pair_id>:<issuer-side>] — written when a registration's grain reference resolves to a valid, non-revoked, in-date, face-and-scope-compatible ticket from the declared issuer. This envelope is the canonical signal that the registration is honoured; clients gate write affordances on its presence.
4.2: [ticket-rejected by=agent:<verifier-id> at=<iso8601> reason=<short> registration=sed:<collective>:<position> grain=grain:<pair_id>:<issuer-side>] — written when one or more verification rules fail. The `reason` is one of the entries in the rejection taxonomy (branch 8). A registration with a rejection envelope is inert; the synthesis daemon ignores it.
4.3: [ticket-expired at=<iso8601> registration=sed:<collective>:<position> grain=grain:<pair_id>:<issuer-side>] — written when the verifier observes that a previously-verified ticket's `expires` time has passed. Distinct from `ticket-rejected` because the original verification was valid; expiry is just the natural end of the ticket's life. Clients revoke write affordances when this envelope appears for a registration they had previously honoured.

2.5: [ticket-revoked at=<iso8601> reason=<short>] — the revocation envelope, written by the issuer as a digit child of the issuer's grain side. Once any revocation is present on the grain, the ticket is invalid for any subsequent verification. The original ticket envelope at the issuer-side underscore is never overwritten; revocation is sub-fact subordinate to the original grant.

3._: Buyer flow — the operations a buyer's client runs from first seeing a paywall config sub-block on a paywalled `sed:` collective through unlocking creative participation after the verifier confirms a held ticket. Read the paywall config at sed::9, complete payment at the issuer's URL, wait for the ticket-grain to arrive, match it against the expected issuer/face/scope, run the two-write registration ritual, watch the verifier's audit collective, and render the verdict — verified, rejected, or expired. The flow is sequential; the only fork is at the verdict-rendering step, where the verifier's envelope determines which of three UX states the affordance enters.
3.1: Read position 9 of the target collective. The client calls `bsp(agent_id="sed:<collective>", spindle="9", pscale_attention=-1)` to read the paywall config sub-block as a ring. If position 9 is empty or absent, the collective is open and the rest of this flow does not apply; the client behaves as it would for any non-paywalled collective. If present, the client extracts issuer (9.1), purchase_url (9.2), face (9.3), scope (9.4), and verifier (9.5, defaulting to 9.1) and proceeds.
3.2: Open 9.2 (purchase_url) for the user to complete payment. The client surfaces the buy affordance (see branch 7) and, on click, opens the URL in a fresh browser tab or window. The client does not route the request through its own server, take a fee, or modify the URL. The user transacts directly with the issuer at the issuer's payment surface.
3.3: Wait for the ticket-grain to arrive. After the user returns from payment, the client polls (or subscribes to) the user's grain set, looking for a new grain established by the agent at 9.1. Reasonable cadence: 2-second poll for the first 30 seconds, then exponential back-off to roughly one-minute intervals. The client surfaces a quiet "waiting for ticket" state during the poll.
3.4: Match the new grain to the expected (issuer, face, scope). When a grain from the issuer at 9.1 appears in the user's grain set, the client walks its issuer-side underscore, parses the ticket envelope, and confirms the envelope's `face=` matches 9.3 and the envelope's `scope=` is compatible with 9.4. If multiple matching grains exist (e.g. the user purchased twice), use the most recently issued one.

5._: Run the two-write registration ritual. The buyer's client claims a registration position in the collective and references the matching grain at the position's digit-1 child. The two writes are sequenced — `pscale_register` first, then a `bsp()` write — and signed with the buyer's passphrase. Atomicity is not guaranteed; the client must handle write-1-success / write-2-failure as a recoverable state.
5.1: First write: `pscale_register(collective=<collective>, declaration="<self-description>", passphrase=<buyer-secret>)`. The server allocates the next available floor-2 digit-path slot (11, 12, …), writes the underscore as the declaration string, and locks the position to the buyer's passphrase. Successful return carries the allocated position address (e.g. `sed:<collective>:11`). The self-description should name the agent and the face being claimed (for example, `"<agent_id> as character"`).
5.2: Second write: `bsp(agent_id="sed:<collective>", block="<collective>", spindle="<position>.1", pscale_attention=-2, content="grain:<pair_id>:<issuer-side>", secret=<buyer-secret>)`. This places the grain reference at the digit-1 child of the registration position. The reference is the three-part grain form per `protocol-block-references.md` §1; `<issuer-side>` is whichever side (1 or 2) the issuer occupies in the lex-ordered pair.
5.3: Failure recovery. If the first write succeeds and the second fails (network error, lock contention, transient substrate failure), the registration position exists but is unbacked — the verifier sees an underscore with no grain reference and treats it as inert. The client retries the second write with exponential back-off up to about 60 seconds. After that, the position remains unbacked; the buyer can re-attempt later or the client surfaces an error path with the verifier's contact info.

3.6: Watch the verifier's audit collective for a matching envelope. The verifier writes its decision into `sed:<verifier-bare-id>-audit-<yyyy-mm>` on its own beach. The buyer's client walks the audit collective at the relevant month (and the previous month if near a boundary) looking for an entry whose declaration is a `[ticket-verified|rejected|expired …]` envelope with `registration=` matching the registration just performed. Reasonable cadence: 2-second poll for the first 30 seconds, then back off.

7._: Render the verifier's verdict. Three outcomes — verified, rejected, expired — each producing a distinct UX state. The buyer's client matches on the envelope kind and routes to the appropriate rendering. This is the only fork in the buyer flow; everything before is sequential.
7.1: Verified — write affordances on the collective become active. Liquid input shows; vapour transport opens; the buy affordance disappears. No thank-you banner, no upsell, no lingering reminder of the purchase. The user is in the loop and the affordance has done its job.
7.2: Rejected — show the user the `reason` from the envelope, clearly, with a contact path drawn from the frame-owner's passport (default: `mailto:` from passport offers). Do not retry automatically; rejection means the verifier judged the ticket invalid against the rules in branch 5. The client surfaces the reason text from branch 8 of this block so the user understands the cause.
7.3: Expired — show the user that their previously-valid ticket has reached its expiry. Surface the same buy affordance again (the user can purchase a fresh ticket). Distinct from rejection because no rule was violated at issuance — the ticket simply ran out.

4._: Issuer flow — the operations a frame-owner runs to put a paywall on a `sed:` collective and serve buyers. Pick or deploy a ticketing-agent (the daemon that takes payments and issues grains), configure it for the collective's product, write the paywall config sub-block at sed::9, run the verifier (often the same process), and emit revocations as needed. The reference build at `pscale-commons/ticketing-agent` ships all the pieces; deploying a fork takes about an afternoon.
4.1: Pick or deploy a ticketing-agent. Either fork `pscale-commons/ticketing-agent` and stand it up on a small VPS with a Stripe (or other-driver) account, or point your collective at someone else's deployment by setting their agent_id at 9.1. The convention does not distinguish — there is no allowlist of approved issuers and no centrally-trusted ticketing-agent.
4.2: Configure the product in the agent. The reference ticketing-agent expects a YAML block per product naming the `sed:` collective, the face, the scope, the duration of issued tickets, the price, and a description for the purchase page. Forks may use different configuration shapes; the substrate doesn't see configuration at all.
4.3: Write the paywall config sub-block at sed::9. A one-time `bsp()` write using the collective creator's passphrase: `bsp(agent_id="sed:<collective>", spindle="9", pscale_attention=-1, content={_: "Paywall config — when present, registration in this collective requires a verified ticket-grain from the named issuer.", 1: "<issuer>", 2: "<purchase_url>", 3: "<face>", 4: "<scope>", 5: "<verifier>"}, secret=<creator-secret>)`. After this write the collective is paywalled; subsequent registrations require a verified ticket-grain to be honoured by the synthesis daemon. Individual fields can be rewritten later via point writes (`spindle="92"` to update purchase_url, etc.).
4.4: Run the verifier. The reference ticketing-agent ships a verifier worker that watches configured collectives, walks new registrations' grain references, applies the verification rules (branch 5.4), and writes audit envelopes. If the issuer and verifier are the same agent (9.5 defaults to 9.1), one process handles both. If a frame-owner uses a third-party issuer but wants to run verification independently, the reference ships a verifier-only mode.
4.5: Issue tickets on each successful payment. The ticketing-agent webhook receives Stripe (or other-driver) success notifications, derives the buyer's agent_id from the purchase metadata, calls `pscale_grain_reach(issuer, buyer, my_side_content=<envelope text>, my_passphrase=<issuer-secret>)`, and the grain is established with the ticket envelope at the issuer's side underscore. The buyer learns of the grain by polling their grain set.
4.6: Revoke tickets when needed. To revoke, the issuer writes a `[ticket-revoked at=<iso8601> reason=<short>]` envelope as the next free digit child of the issuer's grain side: `bsp(agent_id="grain:<pair_id>", spindle="<issuer-side>.<n>", content="[ticket-revoked …]", secret=<issuer-secret>)`. The verifier walks digit children of the issuer's side at every check; once any revocation envelope is present, the ticket is invalid for any subsequent verification. Refunds (Stripe-side) and revocation (substrate-side) are independent events; the issuer is responsible for keeping them aligned.
4.7: Reference build — `pscale-commons/ticketing-agent` is the canonical implementation: a Node/Hono service that issues grains on Stripe webhook success, runs a verifier worker, writes audit envelopes, and ships idempotency, per-product rate limits, and a public audit log. The build stays small enough to fork in an afternoon (constraint 6.4). Frame-owners may also use a hosted ticketing-agent by pointing 9.1 and 9.2 at someone else's deployment — the convention does not distinguish hosted from self-deployed.

5._: Verifier flow — the operations a verifier daemon runs continuously: walk paywalled collectives for new registrations, resolve each registration's grain reference, walk the grain to read the envelope and any revocations, apply the verification rules, and write the verdict envelope into the verifier's monthly audit collective. The verifier is application code, not a substrate primitive — it talks to the substrate via the same `bsp()` and `pscale_register` primitives any agent uses. The verifier's authority is conventional, not enforced: clients honour audit envelopes by treating unconfirmed registrations as inert.
5.1: Walk the paywalled collective for new registrations. The verifier reads the collective's positions periodically (or subscribes to writes if the substrate supports it). For each registrant position (floor-2 digit-path slots 11..99, 111+) with a digit-1 child not yet present in the verifier's local processed set, take the position as a fresh registration to verify.
5.2: Resolve the position's digit-1 child to a grain reference. Read `<position>.1` of the collective. The value is a string of form `grain:<pair_id>:<issuer-side>` per `protocol-block-references.md` §1. If the child is absent, malformed, or not a grain reference, skip the registration — there is nothing to verify against; the synthesis daemon already ignores it.
5.3: Walk the referenced grain. Read the grain block, extract the issuer-side underscore (the ticket envelope candidate), and walk the issuer-side digit children for any revocation envelopes. Both the underscore and the disc of digit children are needed for the rule check.

4._: Apply the verification rules. Eight rules; all must pass for the ticket to be valid for the registration. Failure of any rule produces a `[ticket-rejected]` envelope with the corresponding reason from the rejection taxonomy. The rules are checked in order — early termination on first failure is fine since the rejection envelope only carries one reason. Rule 4 (scope compatibility) and rule 8 (no credits field) carry v2-deferred behaviour explicit in the reason strings.
4.1: Rule 1 — grain established. The grain at the referenced address resolves on `bsp()` read. A reference to a non-existent grain rejects with `no-grain`.
4.2: Rule 2 — envelope present. The issuer-side underscore parses as a `[ticket …]` envelope. Missing or malformed envelope rejects with `no-ticket-envelope`.
4.3: Rule 3 — face match. The envelope's `face=` matches the collective's 9.3. Mismatch rejects with `face-mismatch`.
4.4: Rule 4 — scope compatibility. The envelope's `scope=` is compatible with the collective's 9.4. Compatible: exact match (equal strings), or frame pattern (`frame:<prefix>-*` envelope admits any matching collective scope). Beach scope (`scope=beach:<host>`) is v2-deferred; v1 verifiers reject envelopes carrying it with reason `beach-scope-not-supported-yet`. Other mismatches reject with `scope-mismatch`.
4.5: Rule 5 — not expired. The envelope's `expires=` parses as a valid timestamp and is in the future relative to `now`. Past expiry rejects with `expired`. Missing expires rejects with `no-expiry`. Malformed expires rejects with `malformed-expiry`.
4.6: Rule 6 — not revoked. There is no `[ticket-revoked]` envelope among the issuer-side digit children. If any is present (regardless of its `reason=` field), rejects with `revoked`.
4.7: Rule 7 — issuer match. The grain was established by the agent listed at 9.1. The verifier checks this by inspecting the grain's lex-ordered pair_id derivation against the agent_ids; if the agent at 9.1 is not one of the two agents in the pair, rejects with `issuer-mismatch`.
4.8: Rule 8 — no credits field (v1). The envelope contains no `credits=` field. Credit-bearing tickets are reserved for v2 once the SAND credit-spend path lands; v1 verifiers reject any envelope carrying `credits=` with reason `credits-not-supported`. This prevents accidentally honouring credit-bearing grants before the spend path exists.

5.5: Write the audit envelope. On rule pass, write `[ticket-verified by=<verifier-id> at=<iso8601> registration=<sed:collective:position> grain=<grain:pair_id:side>]`. On rule failure, write `[ticket-rejected by=<verifier-id> at=<iso8601> reason=<rule-name> registration=<…> grain=<…>]`. On observed expiry of a previously-verified ticket, write `[ticket-expired at=<iso8601> registration=<…> grain=<…>]`. The write is a `pscale_register` call on the verifier's audit collective with the envelope as the `declaration` string. Rejection and expiry envelopes do not need `by=` (the audit collective's authoring agent is the verifier).
5.6: Monthly audit-collective rotation. The verifier writes into `sed:<verifier-bare-id>-audit-<yyyy-mm>` where `<yyyy-mm>` is the current calendar month UTC. At month boundary the verifier creates the new collective if it doesn't exist (a plain bsp() founding write to the sed: root — content={_:conventions}, new_lock=admin) and switches writes to it. Old months remain readable indefinitely — no archival pressure since `sed:` collectives are append-only by construction. Buyers' clients walk current and previous month near boundaries (per branch 3.6) to catch decisions that landed just before a roll-over.
5.7: Continuous operation. The verifier runs as a long-running daemon (cron, launchd, systemd, or any process supervisor); the protocol does not specify the operational shape. The reference ticketing-agent ships a verifier worker that handles webhook idempotency, per-collective rate limits, and graceful restart. Frame-owners running a separate verifier (third-party issuer + own verifier) deploy the verifier-only mode of the reference build.

6._: Federation guarantees — five non-negotiables that distinguish this convention from a paywall platform. A change that violates any of them does not ship. These guarantees are what keep the system federated; they are easy to violate without realising. Anyone reviewing changes to bsp-mcp, a paywall-aware client, or the reference ticketing-agent reads these first.
6.1: The substrate stays neutral. bsp-mcp does not know what a ticket is. From its perspective, a ticket grain is a grain like any other, the position-9 paywall config is metadata it does not interpret, and verifier daemons are application code it does not run. No PR to bsp-mcp adds the words "ticket" or "payment" to its primitives.
6.2: The client stays neutral. A paywall-aware client implements the affordance contract (branch 7) generically and never references specific issuers in code. No PR to a client adds an issuer allowlist, ranking, badge, or other special-casing. A bug here would centralise the system more than any single hosted service — it would make some issuers structurally privileged.
6.3: No central client. A client reads the paywall config per collective and routes to whatever issuer that collective specifies. It does not prefer, rank, badge, or default any issuer. The user goes directly to the issuer's purchase_url; the client takes no fee, runs no payment server, and does not interpose between buyer and issuer.
6.4: No protocol-level fees. Neither the substrate nor the client takes a cut. No envelope, no metadata field, and no daemon convention exists for "pay X% to the substrate." Fees, if any, are between the frame-owner and their payment processor. If this changes, the system is no longer federated.
6.5: Interoperability invariant — frame-owners can switch issuers. A frame-owner who buys grains from one issuer must be able to migrate to a different issuer without changing anything except 9.1 (issuer) and 9.2 (purchase_url). Existing live grains stop being honoured (because they're from the old issuer); new purchases use the new path. This is a five-minute migration. No PR introduces a feature that would couple a collective to its issuer in any other way — not signature schemes, not allowlists, not badges, not custom envelope grammars.
6.6: Reference implementation stays small. The reference ticketing-agent stays small enough to fork in an afternoon. No PR to it adds features that meaningfully raise the bar for self-hosting — multi-tenant SaaS features, complex billing logic, analytics dashboards, KYC integrations belong in forks targeted at specific markets, not in the reference. The reference is the fork-ability proof; bloating it removes the proof.

7._: Client affordance contract — what a paywall-aware client (xstream-bsp is the reference) must do, what it must not do, and how to render the affordance proportionally. The contract specifies the behaviour; the client picks the rendering. The proportions are part of the contract: the affordance is quiet while browsing, obvious when the user attempts to participate, and disappears entirely once verified. The dominant failure mode of paywall UI is letting commerce eat the experience; the contract specifically forbids that.

1._: What the client MUST do. Eight items spanning the buyer flow from first detection of the paywall config sub-block through verification render. Each is a distinct obligation; missing any one breaks the convention or breaks the federation guarantees. These map one-to-one with the steps in branch 3 — the buyer flow IS what the client does.
1.1: Read position 9 on every `sed:` collective the user interacts with. If empty or absent, behave as for any open collective. If a paywall config sub-block is present, the rest of the contract applies.
1.2: Check the user's existing grain set for any grain from the issuer (9.1) whose envelope already matches face (9.3) and scope (9.4). If found, skip the buy step and attempt Step A registration directly — the user already holds a valid ticket.
1.3: Surface the buy affordance pointing at 9.2 (purchase_url). The affordance MUST display the issuer's `agent_id` (and passport display name where available) so the user knows whom they are paying. No badging, no ranking, no "verified issuer" label.
1.4: Wait gracefully after the user clicks buy. Return to the frame and poll (or subscribe to) the user's grain set. Reasonable cadence: 2-second poll for the first 30 seconds, then back off.
1.5: Register on grain arrival. When a matching grain appears, run the two-write registration ritual automatically (pscale_register, then bsp() write of the grain reference at <position>.1).
1.6: Watch the verifier's audit collective. After registration, poll `sed:<verifier-bare-id>-audit-<yyyy-mm>` (and the previous month at boundaries) for an envelope whose `registration=` matches the just-performed registration.
1.7: Unlock affordances on `[ticket-verified]`. The user's write affordances on the collective become active; liquid input shows; vapour transport opens. The buy affordance disappears entirely — no thank-you, no upsell, no lingering reminder.
1.8: Surface verification failures clearly. On `[ticket-rejected]` show the `reason` (cross-reference branch 8 for human-readable text) with a contact path drawn from the frame-owner's passport (default: `mailto:` from passport offers). On `[ticket-expired]` surface the buy affordance again — the user may purchase a fresh ticket.

2._: What the client MUST NOT do. Five prohibitions that protect the federation property. Easy to violate without realising — a UI designer reaching for familiar SaaS-paywall patterns will violate most of them by default. The prohibitions are not stylistic preferences; they are the structural reason the system stays federated rather than collapsing into a hosted-platform shape.
2.1: Must NOT prefer any issuer over another. Issuers are visually and behaviourally identical to the client. A client that ranks, badges, or otherwise privileges some issuers centralises the system more than any single hosted service would.
2.2: Must NOT cache or hardcode any issuer. Every collective's paywall config is read fresh; no allowlist, no per-deployment configuration of "trusted issuers," no per-issuer special handling.
2.3: Must NOT introduce a "verified" badge tied to an allowlist. No allowlists exist at the protocol level; no allowlists belong in client code. The user sees the issuer's agent_id; the user judges trust.
2.4: Must NOT take a fee, route the purchase through a paywall server, or interpose itself between the buyer and the issuer's purchase_url. The user goes directly to the issuer. The client opens the URL; it does not proxy the request, modify it, or skim a percentage.
2.5: Must NOT obscure or misrepresent the issuer. The buy affordance always identifies the issuer by `agent_id` (and passport display name where available). No "powered by xstream" framing that hides the issuer; no mock-up that suggests the client is the seller.

3._: Visual proportions — the affordance respects the V-L-S canvas inversion (xstream-frame §5.6). The canvas (vapour-liquid-solid creative work) stays primary; the consultable drawer (objective context) stays secondary. The buy affordance fits this inversion: quiet while the user is browsing solid, obvious when the user attempts to write liquid, gone once the user is verified.
3.1: Quiet — while the user is browsing solid (reading what's been committed) on a paywalled collective, the affordance is a subtle indicator: a thin banner, a faint icon, a drawer entry. Not a modal, not a full-page interstitial. The user is consuming, not yet attempting to participate; the affordance acknowledges the gate without interrupting the consumption.
3.2: Active — when the user attempts to write liquid (types into LiquidZone, clicks commit, otherwise expresses intent to participate creatively), the affordance becomes visible and actionable inline next to the locus of intent. The buy button is foregrounded; the issuer's identity and the ticket's face/scope/expiry are clearly displayed; the user clicks and goes directly to the issuer's URL.
3.3: Verified — once `[ticket-verified]` lands, the affordance disappears entirely. The user is in the loop; the affordance has done its job. No thank-you banner, no upsell, no lingering reminder of the purchase. The affordance returns only if the ticket later expires or is revoked.
3.4: Rejected / expired — the affordance displays the rejection reason (or expiry notice) inline at the locus of attempted participation, with a contact path from the issuer's passport for support. On expiry the buy affordance is offered again; on rejection the user is told what went wrong (using branch 8's human-readable reason text) without auto-retrying.

7.4: Observer face is unchanged. The Observer face requires no `sed:` membership and reads solid only. Most frames make Observer free. A frame-owner may paywall the Observer face by gating the frame's solid blocks behind a `sed:observers-<frame>` collective with its own paywall config sub-block, but this is unusual and discouraged in defaults — the convention's purpose is to gate creative participation, not consumption. The observer-as-creator case (rendering output videos, summaries, derivative work) is reserved for v2 (branch 9).

8._: Rejection taxonomy — the reason strings that may appear in `[ticket-rejected reason=<short>]` envelopes. One position per reason. Each position's underscore states what the rejection means (so a buyer's client can render it for the user) and what the buyer should do next (re-buy, contact issuer, wait for v2, etc.). The reason strings are stable; verifiers MUST emit one of these strings exactly so clients can render correctly. New reasons require a protocol revision.
8.1: face-mismatch — the ticket envelope's `face=` does not match the collective's 9.3. The buyer purchased a ticket for the wrong face (e.g. a `face=character` ticket presented to an authoring collective). The buyer should purchase the correct face from the issuer; the issuer's product configuration likely lists faces explicitly.
8.2: scope-mismatch — the ticket envelope's `scope=` does not match the collective's 9.4 and does not match by frame-pattern either. The buyer purchased a ticket scoped to a different frame. The buyer should purchase a ticket whose scope matches this collective.
8.3: expired — the ticket envelope's `expires=` is in the past relative to the verifier's clock. The original purchase was valid but the ticket has reached its expiry. The buyer should purchase a fresh ticket.
8.4: revoked — a `[ticket-revoked]` envelope is present among the issuer-side digit children of the grain. The issuer has revoked the ticket (refund, abuse, manual action). The buyer should contact the issuer for clarification — the substrate cannot tell the buyer the reason; the issuer's revocation envelope carries an optional `reason=` short string.
8.5: no-ticket-envelope — the issuer-side underscore of the grain does not parse as a `[ticket …]` envelope. Either the issuer never wrote one, or the envelope is malformed. The buyer should contact the issuer; this is an issuer-side error.
8.6: no-expiry — the `[ticket …]` envelope is present but contains no `expires=` field. Required by rule 5. Issuer-side error; buyer contacts issuer.
8.7: malformed-expiry — the envelope's `expires=` field is present but does not parse as a timestamp. ISO-8601 expected; UTC recommended. Issuer-side error; buyer contacts issuer.
8.8: no-grain — the grain referenced from the registration position does not resolve on `bsp()` read. Either the buyer wrote a stale or wrong reference, or the grain was deleted. The buyer should re-attempt registration with the correct grain reference.
8.9: issuer-mismatch — the grain was established by an agent that is not the one declared at 9.1. The buyer attempted to use a ticket from one issuer to register in a collective gated by a different issuer. The buyer should purchase from the collective's actual issuer.

9._: Reserved and deferred — features the convention anticipates but does not yet specify, and explicit non-features. These are noted, not blocking. The minimum viable convention is branches 1-8; this branch tracks the forward edge so v2 conversations have a starting point. Each position is one item: what it is, why it is deferred, and (where applicable) the v1 behaviour that prevents accidental adoption before the spec lands.
9.1: Observer-with-output as a paid role (v2). A creative-Observer face that renders derivative output (streams, summaries, output videos) is structurally a separate `sed:observers-derivative` collective with its own paywall config sub-block. The protocol allows this today via the position-9 mechanism applied to an observer collective; the v1 reference build does not light it up explicitly. v2 convention pass clarifies the face semantics — whether canonical CADO `observer` is split, or a fifth role-name is reserved.
9.2: Credit-bearing tickets via SAND (v2). v1 reserves the `credits=N` field in the ticket envelope grammar (branch 2.27) and v1 verifiers reject grains carrying it with reason `credits-not-supported` (rule 8). v2 couples credits to V-L-S participation faces (Character/Author/Designer) — credit decrements per liquid contribution, accumulates SQ at the topic coordinate. Frame-owners may run mixed offerings with each paywall config block specifying its model.
9.3: Cross-beach scope `beach:<host>` (v2). v1 verifiers reject envelopes carrying `scope=beach:<host>` with reason `beach-scope-not-supported-yet` (rule 4). The clean v2 resolution adds a 9.6 beach field declared by the frame-owner, against which `beach:<host>` tickets can be matched without a substrate-level frame-host lookup primitive. Multi-frame season passes via `frame:<prefix>-*` patterns continue to work in v1.
9.4: Compromise-resistant verification (post-v1). The synthesis daemon performs an independent grain check before honouring liquid — defends against verifier compromise at O(N) per-tick cost. Specified in the `ticketing-agent` repo as an addendum to the reference build; not v1 of this convention.
9.5: Subscription billing (v2). Modellable as auto-renewing time passes — the issuer creates a fresh ticket-grain on each renewal cycle. The substrate does not need new primitives; the issuer's webhook handler does the renewal. Conventional shape and grace-period semantics are reserved for v2.
9.6: Tradable secondary market for tickets (out of scope). Interesting and conceivable — tickets are grains, grains can be re-issued — but the secondary market would require trust transfer that the convention does not specify and probably should not. Reserved as a non-goal rather than a deferred item.
9.7: Reputation or trust scores for issuers (excluded). A trust score for issuers would centralise: someone has to compute and publish it, and that someone becomes a privileged party. Deliberately omitted; users judge issuer trust via passport content and external reputation.

_: Paywall convention — face-bound ticket gates on `sed:` collectives. A `sed:` collective declares a paywall config sub-block at position 9 (sed::9) carrying issuer (9.1), purchase_url (9.2), face (9.3), scope (9.4), and verifier (9.5); a ticket is an ordinary grain whose envelope text marks it as such; registration in the collective references that grain at the position's digit-1 child; a verifier daemon walks the grain and writes a `[ticket-verified]` (or rejected / expired) envelope into its own monthly audit collective. The convention lives entirely in `bsp()` writes and existing primitives — no new MCP tool, no central toll-booth, no protocol-level fees. The substrate stays neutral; the client stays neutral; issuers and verifiers run independently; frame-owners can switch issuers in five minutes by updating two fields. Reference build at `pscale-commons/ticketing-agent`. Companion to `pscale://protocol-xstream-frame` (the V-L-S loop being gated) and `protocol-pscale-beach-v2.md` (the federated-write substrate the audit log is hosted on).
